title: Welsh cakes
subtitle: Griddle-cooked spiced currant cakes from Wales.
excerpt: A soft, slightly crumbly currant cake dough cooked directly on a cast-iron griddle or skillet without any fat, producing small golden rounds that are crisp outside and tender within. Dusted with caster sugar and eaten warm. Makes about twenty.
sourceNotes: Welsh cakes (picau ar y maen). The griddle cake tradition in Wales is ancient, predating domestic ovens. Welsh cakes appear in Welsh domestic cookbooks from the 19th century. The name 'picau ar y maen' means 'cakes on the stone', referring to the flat bakestone or griddle used for cooking them. Mrs Beeton's later editions include Welsh cakes, and the recipe is considered a fundamental part of Welsh culinary heritage.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze cooked Welsh cakes. Warm in a dry pan for 2 minutes per side.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten warm on the day, but keep well for 3 days in a tin.
recipe.temperatureNote: Cook on a medium-low griddle; no oven required.

content.text: Welsh cakes are cooked without fat on the griddle, which is why the cast-iron needs to be properly preheated. If the pan is too hot, the outside scorches before the inside is cooked through; too cool and they do not colour. A medium-low heat and patience is the correct approach. Cook each batch for 3 to 4 minutes per side.

Rub {{unsalted-butter}} of cold butter into {{self-raising-flour}} of flour, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar, {{mixed-spice}} of mixed spice and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt until the mixture resembles fine crumbs. Add {{currants}} of currants. Mix in {{eggs}} beaten egg and {{whole-milk}} of milk to form a soft but not sticky dough. Roll out on a floured surface to about 7 mm thick. Cut out rounds with a 6 cm cutter.

Heat a dry cast-iron skillet or griddle over medium-low heat for 5 minutes. Cook the Welsh cakes in batches for 3 to 4 minutes per side until golden brown and cooked through. Dust with caster sugar while still warm.

items.fix: Medium-low is correct. Welsh cakes need slow, even heat to cook through. If the outside is colouring faster than 3 minutes, reduce the heat.

items.cause: Too little fat, or overworked dough.
items.fix: Mix until the dough just comes together. Overworking develops the gluten and toughens the texture.

items.cause: Currants on the underside of the cake touching the hot pan.
items.fix: This is normal with dried fruit. Cook at medium-low heat and accept a few blackened currants as part of the result.

content.text: Welsh cakes are one of the oldest baking traditions in Britain, cooked on a flat stone before domestic ovens existed. They belong specifically to Wales and to the griddle tradition, appearing at markets, tea rooms and kitchen tables throughout the country. Eaten warm with a dusting of sugar, they are entirely unlike anything else: not a scone, not a biscuit, not a pancake. A thing in its own category.
